title: Scaling features for the stress fluctuations in the OFC model
authors: Naveen Kumar, Rahul Chhimpa, Avinash Chand Yadav
abstract: We consider the Olami-Feder-Christensen (OFC) model on a square two-dimensional lattice with open boundary conditions. The model exhibits self-organized criticality and explains the Gutenberg-Richter law observed for earthquakes. A parameter $α$ controls the level of local dissipation: $α< 0.25$ corresponds to locally dissipative and $α= 0.25$ marks locally conservative dynamics. The avalanche size distribution follows a decaying power-law, with a non-universal critical exponent. Here, we examine the probability distribution of the difference between avalanche size and area. This quantity remains unexplored despite being of significant interest in earthquakes. We find a power-law with a scaling exponent close to one in the conservative OFC model. The scaling feature vanishes even for the physically relevant case $α= 0.21$. To examine the robustness of such features, we also examine the same quantity in the BTW and Manna sandpile models on a square lattice. We find that the power-law behavior survives for these systems due to locally conservative dynamics. We further examine the local and total stress fluctuations in the OFC model for both locally conservative and dissipative dynamics. The finite-size scaling analysis of the power spectra for the stress fluctuations reveals qualitatively the same but quantitatively significantly different behavior. The dynamic exponent describing the divergence of the correlation time with system size changes from nearly ballistic in the conservative to diffusive behavior in the locally dissipative dynamics with $α= 0.21$. The local stress also exhibits a signature of nearly canonical $1/f$ noise in the intermediate regime, and $1/f^2$-type scaling dominates the high-frequency regime.
summary: This paper explores new scaling properties in the OFC model of earthquakes, finding that a power-law distribution exists for the difference between avalanche size and area only in the conservative limit. It also demonstrates how local dissipation fundamentally changes the dynamic exponent of stress fluctuations from ballistic to diffusive behavior.
main_problem: The study investigates new scaling features in the Olami-Feder-Christensen (OFC) model, specifically the probability distribution of the difference between avalanche size and area, and the power spectra of stress fluctuations.
main_result: In the conservative OFC model, the difference between avalanche size and area follows a power-law distribution, a feature that vanishes in the dissipative regime. Additionally, the dynamic exponent shifts from nearly ballistic to diffusive behavior as dissipation increases, with local stress exhibiting 1/f noise.
method: Numerical simulations using an efficient algorithm with active site tracking and 1D array conversion, combined with finite-size scaling (FSS) analysis and power spectra analysis via Fast Fourier Transform (FFT).
model_or_system: The Olami-Feder-Christensen (OFC) model, a cellular automata model of seismic activity on a 2D square lattice with open boundary conditions, which exhibits self-organized criticality.
key_observables: Avalanche size (S), area (A), difference (X = S - A), duration (T), local and total stress fluctuations, and power spectra.
important_parameters: Dissipation parameter (alpha), where alpha = 0.25 is locally conservative and alpha < 0.25 is locally dissipative.
main_assumptions: The model assumes uniform driving and specific redistribution rules that distinguish it from standard sandpile models.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows critical exponents as a function of alpha; Figure 2 shows the probability distribution and data collapse of X; Figure 3 shows the impact of decreasing alpha on P(X, L); Figure 4 compares X scaling in BTW and Manna models; Figures 5 and 6 show stress signals and their power spectra.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the OFC model and its parameters, presents the analysis of the size-area difference (X), compares these findings to BTW and Manna models, analyzes the power spectra of stress fluctuations, and concludes with the implications of dissipation on dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. The OFC model on a square lattice: The critical exponents characterizing avalanche properties in the OFC model on a square lattice with α, a parameter capturing the level of local dissipation. When α = 0.25, the dynamics are locally conservative. τS and τT are the avalanche size and duration exponents, and γST = (τT −1)/(τS −1) describes scaling behavior of average size with duration. When the local dissipation increases (or α decreases), γST increases. We also note that τA ≈τS, where τA is the avalanche area exponent.

Figure 2. The OFC model: (a) The probability distribution for the size area difference X = S−A for different system size L with locally conservative OFC dynamics α = 0.25. The total number of avalanches is 109. About 61.25% of events for L7 have X = 0 or the same size and area values. The dashed straight line guides the slope value. (b) The data collapse plot with DX = 3.1 and DXτX = 3.3. The estimated value of the size area difference exponent is τX = 1.06.

Figure 3. Same as figure 2(a) but with different values of the dissipation parameter α. The system size is L = 27. Notice that the power-law scaling feature of X disappears when the local dissipation level drastically increases.
Figure 4. Same as figure 2(b) but for the BTW and Manna sandpile models on a square lattice. The system size values and the number of avalanches are the same as in figure 2(a).
Figure 5. For the OFC model with α = 0.25: (a) A typical signal for the total stress fluctuations η(t) with a system size L = 26. (b) A portion of the local stress fluctuations.
